In ceremonial contexts, they outranked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered ranking system, with a provisional rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}} * {{ill|Female official|ja|女官}}

'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|ja|明史}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|ja|王政君}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outranked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered ranking system, with a provisional rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}} * {{ill|Female official|ja|女官}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|ja|明史}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|ja|王政君}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outranked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered ranking system, with a provisional rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}} * {{ill|Female official|ja|女官}}

Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|ja|王政君}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outranked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered ranking system, with a provisional rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}} * {{ill|Female official|ja|女官}}

'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/qq173/18qq |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=qq173/18qq |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|ja|明史}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|ja|王政君}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outranked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered ranking system, with a provisional rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}} * {{ill|Female official|ja|女官}}

== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/qq173/18qq |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=qq173/18qq |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|ja|明史}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|ja|王政君}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outranked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered ranking system, with a provisional rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}} * {{ill|Female official|ja|女官}}

'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/qq173/18qq |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=qq173/18qq |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|ja|明史}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/qq173/18qq |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=qq173/18qq |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|ja|明史}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|ja|王政君}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outranked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered ranking system, with a provisional rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}} * {{ill|Female official|ja|女官}}

Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outranked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered ranking system, with a provisional rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}} * {{ill|Female official|ja|女官}}

'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/qq173/18qq |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=qq173/18qq |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|ja|明史}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|ja|王政君}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outranked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered ranking system, with a provisional rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}} * {{ill|Female official|ja|女官}}

'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/qq173/18qq |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=qq173/18qq |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|ja|明史}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|ja|王政君}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outranked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered ranking system, with a provisional rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.

'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|ja|明史}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|ja|王政君}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outranked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered ranking system, with a provisional rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.

== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.

'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|ja|明史}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|ja|王政君}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outranked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered ranking system, with a provisional rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}} * {{ill|Female official|ja|女官}}

Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|ja|王政君}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outranked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered ranking system, with a provisional rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.

'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|ja|明史}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|ja|王政君}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outranked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered ranking system, with a provisional rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.

revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|ja|明史}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|ja|王政君}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outranked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered ranking system, with a provisional rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮}} * {{ill|Female official|ja|女官}}
<!--Miyabito-->
<!--Miyabito-->
<!--Miyabito-->
<!--Miyabito-->
<!--Miyabito-->
<!--Miyabito-->
<!--Miyabito-->
<!--Miyabito-->
<!--Miyabito-->
<!--Miyabito-->

In ceremonial contexts, they outranked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令|12=draft}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=draft}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|12=draft}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|12=draft}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|12=draft}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|12=draft}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered ranking system, with a provisional rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|12=draft}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center|12=draft}}, which issued rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|12=draft}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|12=draft}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|12=draft}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|12=draft}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮|12=draft}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|12=draft}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|12=draft}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義|12=draft}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|12=draft}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|Female official|ja|女官|12=draft}}

'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=draft}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|12=draft}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|ja|明史|12=draft}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|ja|王政君|12=draft}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変|12=draft}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后|12=draft}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outranked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令|12=draft}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=draft}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|12=draft}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|12=draft}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|12=draft}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|12=draft}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered ranking system, with a provisional rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|12=draft}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center|12=draft}}, which issued rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|12=draft}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|12=draft}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|12=draft}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|12=draft}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮|12=draft}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|12=draft}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|12=draft}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義|12=draft}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|12=draft}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|Female official|ja|女官|12=draft}}

'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=draft}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|12=draft}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|ja|明史|12=draft}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|ja|王政君|12=draft}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変|12=draft}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后|12=draft}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令|12=draft}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=draft}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|12=draft}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|12=draft}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|12=draft}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|12=draft}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|12=draft}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center|1=Ministry of the Center|12=draft}}, which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|12=draft}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|12=draft}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|12=draft}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|12=draft}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮|12=draft}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|12=draft}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|12=draft}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義|12=draft}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|12=draft}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|Female official|ja|女官|12=draft}}

}}

However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
