revision.comment: 28 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:宮人 up to rev 101578556
revision.text:
'''宮人'''（きゅうじん/くにん）とは、[[律令制]]において[[宮中]]に奉仕する女性職員のこと。
== 中国における宮人 == 明代において当初『明太祖実録』（巻三十四）などでは「宮人」は[[後宮]]の女性全般を指す言葉だった<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref>。その後、[[洪武]]5年に宮人の冠服が制定され（『[[明史]]』巻六十六）、同時期の女官制度の職掌には宮人の名前・戸籍・給金の管理が定められている（『[[明史]]』巻七十四）<ref name="maeda" />。このような制度化によって「宮人」は后妃や女官以外の後宮女性を指すようになった<ref name="maeda" />。
宮女は主に「良家子」から選抜された。いわゆる良家子は医者、巫者、商人、手工業者を除いて士民を指した（この4つの職業は下流階級と扱われている）（『史記索隠』）。また戦争や家族犯罪で宮女になることも少なくない。宮女の中で資質が優れている人は、女官に昇格することができた。あるいは皇帝に気に入られれば、后妃に取り立てられる例もある。それ以外にも[[王政君]]の例のように、皇帝から皇族に与えられてその妻妾になる例も多い。
多くの宮人は過酷な境遇に置かれており、[[嘉靖帝]]が襲撃される壬寅宮変（[[嘉靖]]21年（1542年））の原因になったといわれている<ref name="maeda" />。一方、[[万暦帝]]の生母（[[孝定太后]]）の李氏に仕えた宮人のように位が二品以上の女性にしか贈られない「夫人」の称号を得た者もいた（『明神宗実録』巻四）<ref name="maeda" />。
皇帝の幼少期に養育係につく乳母や保母についても宮人を冊封する場合が多く、後に皇帝から「夫人」の称号を得た者は冠服が宮人とは異なり、皇帝の関わる冠婚葬祭の席次では女官よりも上位となった<ref name="maeda" />。しかし、皇帝から厚遇を受けた場合でも、皇帝の死とともに権力を失うことが多かった<ref name="maeda" />。
== 日本における宮人 == 日本の後宮制度は[[飛鳥浄御原令]]の頃に整備された。
[[大宝律令|大宝令]]・[[養老令]]の[[後宮職員令]]には「宮人職員」として[[後宮十二司]]の[[職事]]と[[散事]]（[[女孺]]・[[采女]]・[[氏女]]）から構成されていた。
宮人には[[官位相当]]が存在していないが、職事は[[四等官]]に相当するとされ、[[季禄]]支給の基準として[[准位]]が設定され、[[縫殿寮]]が考課を審査、これに基づいて[[中務省]]が[[叙位]]と[[位記]]を発給した。ただし、采女は[[采女司]]の管轄であった。
また、後宮以外にも[[東宮職]]や[[斎宮寮]]には、それぞれ[[東宮]]・[[斎宮]]に仕える宮人がおり、その管理はそれぞれの官司が行った。
[[大化]]以前より[[律令制]]前期まで、後に「[[内裏]]」と称された[[ヤマト大王|大王]]・[[天皇]]の日常空間には男性官人は自由に出入りできなかったために[[奏上]]・[[宣下]]のことは女性である宮人を通じて行われるのが普通であった。
ところが、[[奈良時代]]（8世紀）後期になると内裏の重要性が増して男性官人が内裏に立ち入ることは珍しくなくなっていった。この時期より、「宮人」という名称に代わって女性官人を意味する「'''[[女官]]'''」という呼称が用いられるようになった。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *[[磯貝正義]]「宮人」（『国史大辞典 4』（吉川弘文館、1984年） ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3） *[[大隅清陽]]「宮人」（『日本史大事典 2』（平凡社、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4）
== 関連項目 == *[[後宮]] *[[女官]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:きゆうしん}} [[Category:日本の律令制]] [[Category:職業別の女性]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[宮人]] to [[Kyūjin]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''宮人'''（きゅうじん/くにん）とは、[[律令制]]において[[宮中]]に奉仕する女性職員のこと。
== 中国における宮人 == 明代において当初『明太祖実録』（巻三十四）などでは「宮人」は[[後宮]]の女性全般を指す言葉だった<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref>。その後、[[洪武]]5年に宮人の冠服が制定され（『[[明史]]』巻六十六）、同時期の女官制度の職掌には宮人の名前・戸籍・給金の管理が定められている（『[[明史]]』巻七十四）<ref name="maeda" />。このような制度化によって「宮人」は后妃や女官以外の後宮女性を指すようになった<ref name="maeda" />。
宮女は主に「良家子」から選抜された。いわゆる良家子は医者、巫者、商人、手工業者を除いて士民を指した（この4つの職業は下流階級と扱われている）（『史記索隠』）。また戦争や家族犯罪で宮女になることも少なくない。宮女の中で資質が優れている人は、女官に昇格することができた。あるいは皇帝に気に入られれば、后妃に取り立てられる例もある。それ以外にも[[王政君]]の例のように、皇帝から皇族に与えられてその妻妾になる例も多い。
多くの宮人は過酷な境遇に置かれており、[[嘉靖帝]]が襲撃される壬寅宮変（[[嘉靖]]21年（1542年））の原因になったといわれている<ref name="maeda" />。一方、[[万暦帝]]の生母（[[孝定太后]]）の李氏に仕えた宮人のように位が二品以上の女性にしか贈られない「夫人」の称号を得た者もいた（『明神宗実録』巻四）<ref name="maeda" />。
皇帝の幼少期に養育係につく乳母や保母についても宮人を冊封する場合が多く、後に皇帝から「夫人」の称号を得た者は冠服が宮人とは異なり、皇帝の関わる冠婚葬祭の席次では女官よりも上位となった<ref name="maeda" />。しかし、皇帝から厚遇を受けた場合でも、皇帝の死とともに権力を失うことが多かった<ref name="maeda" />。
== 日本における宮人 == 日本の後宮制度は[[飛鳥浄御原令]]の頃に整備された。
[[大宝律令|大宝令]]・[[養老令]]の[[後宮職員令]]には「宮人職員」として[[後宮十二司]]の[[職事]]と[[散事]]（[[女孺]]・[[采女]]・[[氏女]]）から構成されていた。
宮人には[[官位相当]]が存在していないが、職事は[[四等官]]に相当するとされ、[[季禄]]支給の基準として[[准位]]が設定され、[[縫殿寮]]が考課を審査、これに基づいて[[中務省]]が[[叙位]]と[[位記]]を発給した。ただし、采女は[[采女司]]の管轄であった。
また、後宮以外にも[[東宮職]]や[[斎宮寮]]には、それぞれ[[東宮]]・[[斎宮]]に仕える宮人がおり、その管理はそれぞれの官司が行った。
[[大化]]以前より[[律令制]]前期まで、後に「[[内裏]]」と称された[[ヤマト大王|大王]]・[[天皇]]の日常空間には男性官人は自由に出入りできなかったために[[奏上]]・[[宣下]]のことは女性である宮人を通じて行われるのが普通であった。
ところが、[[奈良時代]]（8世紀）後期になると内裏の重要性が増して男性官人が内裏に立ち入ることは珍しくなくなっていった。この時期より、「宮人」という名称に代わって女性官人を意味する「'''[[女官]]'''」という呼称が用いられるようになった。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *[[磯貝正義]]「宮人」（『国史大辞典 4』（吉川弘文館、1984年） ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3） *[[大隅清陽]]「宮人」（『日本史大事典 2』（平凡社、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4）
== 関連項目 == *[[後宮]] *[[女官]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:きゆうしん}} [[Category:日本の律令制]] [[Category:職業別の女性]]

'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=draft}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|12=draft}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|ja|明史|12=draft}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|ja|王政君|12=draft}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変|12=draft}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后|12=draft}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令|12=draft}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=draft}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|12=draft}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|12=draft}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|12=draft}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|12=draft}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|12=draft}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center|1=Ministry of the Center|12=draft}}, which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|12=draft}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|12=draft}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|12=draft}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|12=draft}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮|12=draft}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|12=draft}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|12=draft}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義|12=draft}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|12=draft}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|Female official|ja|女官|12=draft}}
{{translated page|ja|宮人|version=101578556|comment=Imported full ja history}}

}}

'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|qq=}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ming Taizu Shilu|qq=}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|ja|明史|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Ming|qq=}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Renyin Palace Rebellion|qq=}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Dowager Xiaoding|qq=}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Kiyomihara Code|qq=}}.

== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Masayoshi|qq=}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|qq=}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|qq=}} * {{ill|Female official|ja|女官|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Female official|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|宮人|version=101578556|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[sn:Kyūjin]] [[sn:Miyabito]] [[ja:宮人]] [[sn:iyabito]] [[en:Kyūjin]]
<!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito-->

'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|qq=}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ming Taizu Shilu|qq=}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|ja|明史|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Ming|qq=}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|ja|王政君|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wang Zhengjun|qq=}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Renyin Palace Rebellion|ja|壬寅宮変|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Renyin Palace Rebellion|qq=}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|ja|孝定太后|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Dowager Xiaoding|qq=}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Kiyomihara Code|qq=}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Code|qq=}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|qq=}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|qq=}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|qq=}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|qq=}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|qq=}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|qq=}} and ratified by the {{ill|Nakatsukasashō|ja|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center|1=Ministry of the Center|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsukasashō|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of the Center|qq=}}, which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|qq=}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|qq=}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|qq=}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|qq=}} or the {{ill|Saigū|ja|斎宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigū|qq=}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara period|qq=}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|qq=}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|qq=}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Masayoshi|qq=}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|qq=}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|qq=}} * {{ill|Female official|ja|女官|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Female official|qq=}}

In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />

Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|qq=}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|qq=}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|qq=}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|qq=}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|qq=}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|qq=}} and ratified by the [[:en:Ministry of the Center|Ministry of the Center]], which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|qq=}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|qq=}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|qq=}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|qq=}} or the [[:en:Saikū|Saigū]], respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|qq=}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|qq=}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.

== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Masayoshi|qq=}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|qq=}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. revision.text:
'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|qq=}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ming Taizu Shilu|qq=}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''[[:en:History of Ming|History of Ming]]'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like [[:en:Wang Zhengjun|Wang Zhengjun]], could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the [[:en:Palace plot of Renyin year|Renyin Palace Rebellion]] (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the [[:en:Empress Dowager Xiaoding|Empress Dowager Xiaoding]] (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]].
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|qq=}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|qq=}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|qq=}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|qq=}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|qq=}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|qq=}} and ratified by the [[:en:Ministry of the Center|Ministry of the Center]], which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|qq=}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|qq=}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|qq=}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|qq=}} or the [[:en:Saikū|Saigū]], respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|qq=}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|qq=}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Masayoshi|qq=}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|qq=}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|qq=}} * [[:en:Lady-in-waiting|Female official]]

'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|qq=}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ming Taizu Shilu|qq=}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''[[:en:History of Ming|History of Ming]]'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like [[:en:Wang Zhengjun|Wang Zhengjun]], could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the [[:en:Palace plot of Renyin year|Renyin Palace Rebellion]] (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the [[:en:Empress Dowager Xiaoding|Empress Dowager Xiaoding]] (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]].
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|qq=}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|qq=}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|qq=}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|qq=}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|qq=}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|qq=}} and ratified by the [[:en:Ministry of the Center|Ministry of the Center]], which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|qq=}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|qq=}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|qq=}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|qq=}} or the [[:en:Saikū|Saigū]], respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|qq=}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|qq=}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Masayoshi|qq=}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|qq=}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|qq=}} * [[:en:Lady-in-waiting|Female official]]

Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|qq=}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|qq=}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|qq=}} or the [[:en:Saikū|Saigū]], respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上奏}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|qq=}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Masayoshi|qq=}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|qq=}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|qq=}} * [[:en:Lady-in-waiting|Female official]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q23047376]] Wikidata name: court lady [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|qq=}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ming Taizu Shilu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''[[:en:History of Ming|History of Ming]]'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like [[:en:Wang Zhengjun|Wang Zhengjun]], could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the [[:en:Palace plot of Renyin year|Renyin Palace Rebellion]] (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the [[:en:Empress Dowager Xiaoding|Empress Dowager Xiaoding]] (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]].
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|qq=}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|qq=}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 散位}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|qq=}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|qq=}} and ratified by the [[:en:Ministry of the Center|Ministry of the Center]], which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 采女}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|qq=}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|qq=}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|qq=}} or the [[:en:Saikū|Saigū]], respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上奏}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|qq=}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Masayoshi|qq=}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|qq=}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|qq=}} * [[:en:Lady-in-waiting|Female official]]

[[Category:Completion tier 4]]
}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q23047376]] Wikidata name: court lady [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|qq=|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ming Taizu Shilu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''[[:en:History of Ming|History of Ming]]'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like [[:en:Wang Zhengjun|Wang Zhengjun]], could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the [[:en:Palace plot of Renyin year|Renyin Palace Rebellion]] (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the [[:en:Empress Dowager Xiaoding|Empress Dowager Xiaoding]] (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]].
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|qq=|1=Gogūshokuinryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109357833}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|qq=|1=Shokuji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611227}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 散位|1=sanni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499495}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|qq=|1=nyoju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10942756}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|qq=|1=Nuidonoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608161}} and ratified by the [[:en:Ministry of the Center|Ministry of the Center]], which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 采女|1=Uneme|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644506}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|qq=|1=Crown Prince's Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526631}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|qq=|1=Saikū-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500403}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|qq=|1=Crown Prince|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10870027}} or the [[:en:Saikū|Saigū]], respectively. By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.

ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|qq=|1=Kiyoharu Ōsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56354277}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|qq=|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}} * [[:en:Lady-in-waiting|Female official]]

<!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--> [[Category:Ritsuryō System of Japan]] [[Category:Women by occupation]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[vi:Cung nữ]] [[zh:宫女]] [[zh-yue:宮女]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q23047376]] Wikidata name: court lady [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|qq=|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ming Taizu Shilu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''[[:en:History of Ming|History of Ming]]'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like [[:en:Wang Zhengjun|Wang Zhengjun]], could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the [[:en:Palace plot of Renyin year|Renyin Palace Rebellion]] (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the [[:en:Empress Dowager Xiaoding|Empress Dowager Xiaoding]] (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]].
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|qq=|1=Gogūshokuinryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109357833}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|qq=|1=Shikijikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611227}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 散位|1=sanni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499495}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|qq=|1=nyoju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10942756}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|qq=|1=Nuidonoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608161}} and ratified by the [[:en:Ministry of the Center|Ministry of the Center]], which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 采女|1=Uneme|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644506}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|qq=|1=Crown Prince's Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526631}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|qq=|1=Saikū-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500403}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|qq=|1=Crown Prince|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10870027}} or the [[:en:Saikū|Saigū]], respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上奏|1=Sōjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358776}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|qq=|1=Senchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452917}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Masayoshi|qq=|1=Masayoshi Isogai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588043}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|qq=|1=Kiyoharu Ōsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56354277}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|qq=|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}} * [[:en:Lady-in-waiting|Female official]]
{{translated page|ja|宮人|version=101578556|comment=Imported full ja history}}

ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|qq=|1=Kiyoharu Ōsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56354277}}. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|qq=|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}} * [[:en:Lady-in-waiting|Female official]]

<!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--> [[Category:Ritsuryō System of Japan]] [[Category:Women by occupation]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[vi:Cung nữ]] [[zh:宫女]] [[zh-yue:宮女]] {{wikidata link|Q23047376}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q23047376]] Wikidata name: court lady [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|qq=|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ming Taizu Shilu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''[[:en:History of Ming|History of Ming]]'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like [[:en:Wang Zhengjun|Wang Zhengjun]], could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the [[:en:Palace plot of Renyin year|Renyin Palace Rebellion]] (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the [[:en:Empress Dowager Xiaoding|Empress Dowager Xiaoding]] (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]].
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|qq=|1=Gogūshokuinryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109357833}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|qq=|1=Shikijikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611227}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 散位|1=sanni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499495}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|qq=|1=nyoju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10942756}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|qq=|1=Nuidonoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608161}} and ratified by the [[:en:Ministry of the Center|Ministry of the Center]], which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 采女|1=Uneme|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644506}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|qq=|1=Crown Prince's Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526631}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|qq=|1=Saikū-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500403}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|qq=|1=Crown Prince|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10870027}} or the [[:en:Saikū|Saigū]], respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上奏|1=Sōjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358776}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|qq=|1=Senchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452917}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.

ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}} * [[:en:Lady-in-waiting|Female official]]

<!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--> [[Category:Ritsuryō System of Japan]] [[Category:Women by occupation]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[vi:Cung nữ]] [[zh:宫女]] [[zh-yue:宮女]] {{wikidata link|Q23047376}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q23047376]] Wikidata name: court lady [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ming Taizu Shilu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''[[:en:History of Ming|History of Ming]]'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like [[:en:Wang Zhengjun|Wang Zhengjun]], could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the [[:en:Palace plot of Renyin year|Renyin Palace Rebellion]] (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the [[:en:Empress Dowager Xiaoding|Empress Dowager Xiaoding]] (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]].
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|1=Gogūshokuinryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109357833}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|1=Shikijikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611227}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 散位|1=sanni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499495}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|1=nyoju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10942756}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|1=Nuidonoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608161}} and ratified by the [[:en:Ministry of the Center|Ministry of the Center]], which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 采女|1=Uneme|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644506}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|1=Crown Prince's Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526631}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|1=Saikū-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500403}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|1=Crown Prince|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10870027}} or the [[:en:Saikū|Saigū]], respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上奏|1=Sōjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358776}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|1=Senchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452917}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Masayoshi|1=Masayoshi Isogai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588043}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|1=Kiyoharu Ōsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56354277}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}} * [[:en:Lady-in-waiting|Female official]]

<!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--><!--Miyabito--> [[Category:Ritsuryō System of Japan]] [[Category:Women by occupation]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[vi:Cung nữ]] [[zh:宫女]] [[zh-yue:宮女]] {{wikidata link|Q23047376}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q23047376]] Wikidata name: court lady [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ming Taizu Shilu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''[[:en:History of Ming|History of Ming]]'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like [[:en:Wang Zhengjun|Wang Zhengjun]], could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the [[:en:Palace plot of Renyin year|Renyin Palace Rebellion]] (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the [[:en:Empress Dowager Xiaoding|Empress Dowager Xiaoding]] (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]].
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|1=Gogūshokuinryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109357833}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|1=Shikijikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611227}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 散位|1=sanni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499495}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|1=nyoju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10942756}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|1=Nuidonoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608161}} and ratified by the [[:en:Ministry of the Center|Ministry of the Center]], which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 采女|1=Uneme|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644506}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|1=Crown Prince's Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526631}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|1=Saikū-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500403}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|1=Crown Prince|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10870027}} or the [[:en:Saikū|Saigū]], respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上奏|1=Sōjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358776}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|1=Senchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452917}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
