<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q23047376]] Wikidata name: court lady [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ming Taizu Shilu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''[[:en:History of Ming|History of Ming]]'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like [[:en:Wang Zhengjun|Wang Zhengjun]], could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the [[:en:Palace plot of Renyin year|Renyin Palace Rebellion]] (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the [[:en:Empress Dowager Xiaoding|Empress Dowager Xiaoding]] (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]].
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|1=Gogūshokuinryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109357833}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|1=Shikijikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611227}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 散位|1=sanni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499495}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|1=nyoju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10942756}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|1=Nuidonoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608161}} and ratified by the [[:en:Ministry of the Center|Ministry of the Center]], which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 采女|1=Uneme|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644506}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|1=Crown Prince's Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526631}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|1=Saikū-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500403}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|1=Crown Prince|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10870027}} or the [[:en:Saikū|Saigū]], respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上奏|1=Sōjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358776}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|1=Senchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452917}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Masayoshi|1=Masayoshi Isogai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588043}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|1=Kiyoharu Ōsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56354277}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}} * [[:en:Lady-in-waiting|Female official]]
{{translated page|ja|宮人|version=101578556|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 4]]
}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh_yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh_yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q23047376]] Wikidata name: court lady [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ming Taizu Shilu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''[[:en:History of Ming|History of Ming]]'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like [[:en:Wang Zhengjun|Wang Zhengjun]], could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the [[:en:Palace plot of Renyin year|Renyin Palace Rebellion]] (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the [[:en:Empress Dowager Xiaoding|Empress Dowager Xiaoding]] (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]].
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|1=Gogūshokuinryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109357833}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|1=Shikijikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611227}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 散位|1=sanni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499495}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|1=nyoju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10942756}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|1=Nuidonoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608161}} and ratified by the [[:en:Ministry of the Center|Ministry of the Center]], which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 采女|1=Uneme|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644506}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|1=Crown Prince's Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526631}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|1=Saikū-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500403}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|1=Crown Prince|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10870027}} or the [[:en:Saikū|Saigū]], respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上奏|1=Sōjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358776}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|1=Senchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452917}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Masayoshi|1=Masayoshi Isogai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588043}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|1=Kiyoharu Ōsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56354277}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}} * [[:en:Lady-in-waiting|Female official]]

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[vi:Cung nữ]] [[zh:宫女]] [[zh-yue:宮女]] {{wikidata link|Q23047376}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ritsuryō System of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Women by occupation]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh_yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh_yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q23047376]] Wikidata name: court lady [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ming Taizu Shilu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''[[:en:History of Ming|History of Ming]]'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like [[:en:Wang Zhengjun|Wang Zhengjun]], could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the [[:en:Palace plot of Renyin year|Renyin Palace Rebellion]] (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the [[:en:Empress Dowager Xiaoding|Empress Dowager Xiaoding]] (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]].
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|1=Gogūshokuinryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109357833}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|1=Shikijikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611227}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 散位|1=sanni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499495}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|1=nyoju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10942756}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|1=Nuidonoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608161}} and ratified by the [[:en:Ministry of the Center|Ministry of the Center]], which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 采女|1=Uneme|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644506}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|1=Crown Prince's Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526631}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|1=Saikū-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500403}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|1=Crown Prince|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10870027}} or the [[:en:Saikū|Saigū]], respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上奏|1=Sōjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358776}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|1=Senchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452917}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.

"宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|1=Kiyoharu Ōsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56354277}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q23047376]] Wikidata name: court lady [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ming Taizu Shilu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|en|History of Ming|WD=Q28763|lt=History of Ming}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|en|Wang Zhengjun|WD=Q701379|lt=Wang Zhengjun}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Palace plot of Renyin year|en|Palace plot of Renyin year|WD=Q10931534|lt=Renyin Palace Rebellion}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|en|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|WD=Q4667238|lt=Empress Dowager Xiaoding}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|1=Gogūshokuinryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109357833}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|1=Shikijikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611227}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 散位|1=sanni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499495}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|1=nyoju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10942756}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|1=Nuidonoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608161}} and ratified by the {{ill|Ministry of the Center|en|Ministry of the Center|WD=Q6867690|lt=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 采女|1=Uneme|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644506}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|1=Crown Prince's Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526631}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|1=Saikū-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500403}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|1=Crown Prince|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10870027}} or the {{ill|Saikū|en|Saikū|WD=Q150845|lt=Saigū}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上奏|1=Sōjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358776}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|1=Senchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452917}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Masayoshi|1=Masayoshi Isogai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588043}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|1=Kiyoharu Ōsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56354277}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}} * {{ill|Lady-in-waiting|en|Lady-in-waiting|WD=Q715222|lt=Female official}}
{{translated page|ja|宮人|version=101578556|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]]

In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />

By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.

"宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|1=Kiyoharu Ōsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56354277}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]]
'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ming Taizu Shilu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|en|History of Ming|WD=Q28763|lt=History of Ming}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|en|Wang Zhengjun|WD=Q701379|lt=Wang Zhengjun}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Palace plot of Renyin year|en|Palace plot of Renyin year|WD=Q10931534|lt=Renyin Palace Rebellion}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|en|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|WD=Q4667238|lt=Empress Dowager Xiaoding}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|1=Gogūshokuinryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109357833}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|1=Shikijikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611227}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 散位|1=sanni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499495}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|1=nyoju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10942756}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|1=Nuidonoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608161}} and ratified by the {{ill|Ministry of the Center|en|Ministry of the Center|WD=Q6867690|lt=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 采女|1=Uneme|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644506}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|1=Crown Prince's Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526631}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|1=Saikū-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500403}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|1=Crown Prince|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10870027}} or the {{ill|Saikū|en|Saikū|WD=Q150845|lt=Saigū}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上奏|1=Sōjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358776}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|1=Senchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452917}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.

"宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|1=Kiyoharu Ōsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56354277}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}} * {{ill|Lady-in-waiting|en|Lady-in-waiting|WD=Q715222|lt=Female official}}

Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|1=Nuidonoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608161}} and ratified by the {{ill|Ministry of the Center|en|Ministry of the Center|WD=Q6867690|lt=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 采女|1=Uneme|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644506}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|1=Crown Prince's Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526631}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|1=Saikū-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500403}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|1=Crown Prince|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10870027}} or the {{ill|Saikū|en|Saikū|WD=Q150845|lt=Saigū}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上奏|1=Sōjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358776}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|1=Senchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452917}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.

"宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|1=Kiyoharu Ōsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56354277}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]]
'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ming Taizu Shilu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|en|History of Ming|WD=Q28763|lt=History of Ming|1=History of Ming}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|en|Wang Zhengjun|WD=Q701379|lt=Wang Zhengjun|1=Wang Zhengjun}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Palace plot of Renyin year|en|Palace plot of Renyin year|WD=Q10931534|lt=Renyin Palace Rebellion|1=Palace plot of Renyin year}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|en|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|WD=Q4667238|lt=Empress Dowager Xiaoding|1=Empress Dowager Xiaoding}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code|1=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|1=Gogūshokuinryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109357833}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|1=Shikijikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611227}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 散位|1=San'i|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499495}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|1=nyoju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10942756}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|1=Q11608161|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608161}} and ratified by the {{ill|Ministry of the Center|en|Ministry of the Center|WD=Q6867690|lt=Ministry of the Center|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 采女|1=Uneme|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644506}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|1=Q11526631|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526631}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|1=Saikū-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500403}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|1=Q10870027|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10870027}} or the {{ill|Saikū|en|Saikū|WD=Q150845|lt=Saigū|1=Saikū}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上奏|1=Q11358776|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358776}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|1=senge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452917}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{ill|Isogai Masayoshi|ja|磯貝正義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Masayoshi|1=Masayoshi Isogai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588043}}. "宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|1=Kiyoharu Ōsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56354277}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5353683}} * {{ill|Lady-in-waiting|en|Lady-in-waiting|WD=Q715222|lt=Female official|1=Lady-in-waiting}}

In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code|1=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|1=Gogūshokuinryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109357833}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|1=Shikijikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611227}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 散位|1=San'i|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499495}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|1=nyoju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10942756}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|1=Q11608161|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608161}} and ratified by the {{ill|Ministry of the Center|en|Ministry of the Center|WD=Q6867690|lt=Ministry of the Center|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 采女|1=Uneme|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644506}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|1=Q11526631|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526631}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|1=Saikū-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11500403}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|1=Q10870027|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10870027}} or the {{ill|Saikū|en|Saikū|WD=Q150845|lt=Saigū|1=Saikū}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上奏|1=Q11358776|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358776}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|1=senge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452917}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.

"宮人" in ''Kokushi Daijiten 4.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1984. ISBN 978-4-642-00504-3. * {{ill|Ōsumi Kiyoyō|ja|大隅清陽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsumi Kiyoyō|1=Kiyoharu Ōsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56354277}}. "宮人" in ''Nihonshi Daijiten 2.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮人]][[vi:Cung nữ]][[zh-yue:宮女]][[zh:宫女]]
'''Kyūjin/Kunin''' (宮人) refers to female officials serving in the imperial court under the [[Ritsuryō system]].
== Kyūjin in China == During the Ming dynasty, the term "Kyūjin" initially referred to all women in the {{ill|Imperial harem|ja|後宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial harem|1=back palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q5353683}}, as documented in works like the ''{{ill|Ming Taizu Shilu|ja|明太祖実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ming Taizu Shilu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' (Volume 34).<ref name="maeda">{{Cite journal|和書|author=前田尚美 |date=2009-03 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11173/1811 |title=明代後宮と后妃・女官制度 |journal=京都女子大学大学院文学研究科研究紀要. 史学編 |ISSN=1349-6018 |publisher=京都女子大学 |volume=008 |pages=27-55 |hdl=11173/1811 |CRID=1050001337577490560}}</ref> However, in the 5th year of Hongwu, attire regulations for Kyūjin were established (''{{ill|History of Ming|en|History of Ming|qid=Q28763|lt=History of Ming|1=History of Ming}}'' Volume 66), and administrative roles for the female officials, such as managing their names, households, and salaries, were defined (''History of Ming'' Volume 74).<ref name="maeda" /> This institutionalization narrowed the term "Kyūjin" to exclude empresses and female officials, designating them specifically as lower-Ranking women of the imperial court.<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin were primarily selected from "good families," which excluded physicians, shamans, merchants, and artisans, as these professions were considered lower class (as per ''Shiji Suoyin''). Women could also become Kyūjin due to war or familial crimes. Talented Kyūjin could be promoted to female officials or, if favored by the emperor, elevated to the status of consorts. Others, like {{ill|Wang Zhengjun|en|Wang Zhengjun|qid=Q701379|lt=Wang Zhengjun|1=Wang Zhengjun}}, could be granted as wives or concubines to imperial family members.
The harsh conditions of Kyūjin life were a contributing factor to incidents like the {{ill|Palace plot of Renyin year|en|Palace plot of Renyin year|qid=Q10931534|lt=Renyin Palace Rebellion|1=Palace plot of Renyin year}} (1542) during the reign of the Jiajing Emperor.<ref name="maeda" /> Conversely, some Kyūjin, such as those serving the {{ill|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|en|Empress Dowager Xiaoding|qid=Q4667238|lt=Empress Dowager Xiaoding|1=Empress Dowager Xiaoding}} (mother of the Wanli Emperor), were granted the honorary title of "Madam," typically reserved for women of second Rank or higher (''Ming Shenzong Shilu'' Volume 4).<ref name="maeda" />
Kyūjin who served as wet nurses or caretakers for the emperor during his childhood were often highly respected, receiving honorary titles and distinctive attire. In ceremonial contexts, they outRanked female officials.<ref name="maeda" /> However, their influence often waned after the emperor's death.<ref name="maeda" />
== Kyūjin in Japan == The Japanese imperial court system for Kyūjin was established around the time of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|qid=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code|1=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}}.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|qid=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2575743}}, Kyūjin were defined in the {{ill|Post of the Palace Women|ja|後宮職員令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Post of the Palace Women|1=Gogūshokuinryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q109357833}}, comprising {{ill|Shokuji|ja|職事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuji|1=Shikijikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11611227}} and {{ill|Sanji|ja|散事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 散位|1=San'i|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11499495}} (such as {{ill|Joju|ja|女孺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joju|1=nyoju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10942756}}, {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneme|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ujinome|ja|氏女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
Kyūjin did not have official court Ranks, but Shokuji positions were equivalent to the four-tiered Ranking system, with a provisional Rank determining their eligibility for seasonal stipends. Their evaluations were overseen by the {{ill|Nuidonoryō|ja|縫殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nuidonoryō|1=Q11608161|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11608161}} and ratified by the {{ill|Ministry of the Center|en|Ministry of the Center|qid=Q6867690|lt=Ministry of the Center|1=Ministry of the Center}}, which issued Rank certificates. However, Uneme were managed by the {{ill|Unemeshi|ja|采女司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unemeshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 采女|1=Uneme|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11644506}}.
Kyūjin also served outside the imperial harem, such as in the {{ill|Crown Prince's Household|ja|東宮職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince's Household|1=Q11526631|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11526631}} or the {{ill|Saigūryō|ja|斎宮寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigūryō|1=Saikū-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11500403}}, where they attended to the {{ill|Crown Prince|ja|東宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crown Prince|1=Q10870027|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10870027}} or the {{ill|Saikū|en|Saikū|qid=Q150845|lt=Saigū|1=Saikū}}, respectively. These roles were administered by their respective bureaus.
Until the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} (8th century), male officials were generally not allowed unrestricted access to the emperor's private quarters, necessitating the mediation of Kyūjin for matters like {{ill|Sōjō|ja|奏上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上奏|1=Q11358776|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11358776}} and {{ill|Senchō|ja|宣下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senchō|1=senge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11452917}}.
By the late Nara period, as the imperial palace gained political significance, male officials began entering the palace more frequently. Consequently, the term "Kyūjin" fell out of use and was replaced by "Nyokan" (女官), denoting female officials.
